author: @stravs8097
text: Bless you for doing this video. I watched Nigel's 1+1+1 video and found it very interesting. I also have put together his so called "quantum battery" (magnet,copper,wet-paper,zinc repeat) - although a different user claiming to be in contact with Nigel said the water needed to be infused with magnesium, and instead of doing that, used magnesium strips after the paper. Nigel said with enough of them one could "power a house". The battery is impressive voltage wise, but like you pointed out, the current is in the uA range. I tried filling capacitors, and that works - if one is willing to wait. A 10V Nigel-battery takes several seconds to fill a 1mF cap. Filling a 1F cap to even 2V from the same battery has so far taken days. So it works, it's just slow. Also, it dries out after a time. As a test, I made the same voltaic stack without the magnets and got the same result, as long as it was pressed together tightly.

author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text:
I built as system a bit like this; but i used copper wire around some steel shapes. I found that if I wound a second coil around the first and then placed a magnet on the side it would output a small voltage. 

This voltage and the amount of current it supplied could be increased significantly if I connected the second winding to the 240V mains. Once connected to the mains the magnet really did nothing sad to say.

I think I’m on to something here and I’m on tenterhooks thinking the video might go viral.

author: @Flashbry
text:
Hi Robert,

A very good video as always!

I have a question that has been rattling around my head for a number of years, that I hope you can answer.
I only have a rudimentary knowledge of electronics and I know you can't create energy from nothing so please let me know if this is impossible.

The problem: 

People overloading their 13a outlets with multiblock plug sockets which then catch fire and in extreme cases burn down the house and anyone unlucky enough to be in it.

My proposed solution: 

A multiblock providing say 4 sockets all providing upto 13a, that can be plugged into a normal 13a socket (so it would appear that the 13a socket is providing 52a (4x13a).

Now I don't know if this is possible, but the way I see it working is that each socket will only be live for 25% of the time.
The multiblock would contain capacitors to enable each socket to ride out the 75% of te time that it is not getting mains power.
I imagine it working a bit like interrupts on a cpu with some sort of timer and switching circuit that would provide power for say 50 milliseconds to the first socket, then cut the power and move onto the next socket until it reaches socket number 4. After providing power to socket number 4 it would then reset to power socket number 1 and so on.
Obviously if it is possible using capacitors then the capacitors would need to be charged either as each socket is powered (the capacitors for each socket only recieve power when their socket is active), or they could be charged regardless of which socket is powered (all the capacitors are always on charge, but they can only discharge to a particular socket) so that every socket has a charged capacitor when it needs it.

Does that make sense? It does in my head but I'm not sure if it is easy to do. 
If it is easy then why (apart from cost) don't multiblocks have this as a feature now? 
I imagine it would save a lot of lives. 
Someone I know recently died due to this problem which is what triggered it again in my mind...

It would be great if a solution could be found and made available to all so that the big companies can't charge an arm and 2 legs for it.

author: @paulwright8378
text: why do we use copper coils of wire instead of steel wire in generators

replies.author: @robinvince616
replies.text: Anything that is producing electricity has an 'internal resistance'. This manifests itself as a resistor in series with its output. When we attempt to a draw some current, it is flowing through that resistor causing the output voltage to drop. Copper is a much better conductor of electricity than steel, so much less power is lost when you attempt to draw current. In a generator there are also other factors contributing to its internal resistance, but it's still worth keeping the coil losses low. Once you are drawing sufficient current for the output voltage to drop to half its off-load value, your generator is wasting as much energy as is going into your load. Now with only half the voltage available, only half the current is flowing, so your load is only running at a quarter of the power you would be getting from a perfect generator (one with no internal resistance). A low internal resistance is thus very important for any source of voltage (battery or generator) when it is required to supply a useful amount of power.

I discovered there is a real world polarity switching between day and night, positive during the day and negative by night, my solar panel reversed polarity and started sucking up water from my water barrel (it was a bubbler by day) - i checked the voltage and it was reading 1.9v (at night!)  - This is worth investigating and is the very reason they fitted blocking diodes in home solar panels - otherwise it would feed energy from your grid directly to the solar panels.

I was emailing Nigel for a short while, even hosted some of his videos as the science interests me, i suggested he look more into Walter Russells work, its not hard to understand once you realise what it all relates to. He doesnt help himself much with his demonstrations so im still waiting to see/hear more about his work, until he is debunked/proven as a scammer hes got my benefit of the doubt.

author: @mtttony
text:
Food for thought!!  And the only investment required is a little of your time. Thank you Robert.
 For over one hundred years people have been trying to split water into Hydrogen and Oxygen with diminishing returns for the energy input.

Without concern to the efficiency of the combustion process of these gases.
Combustion of Hydrogen and Oxygen only consumes one millionth of these gases.
Most of the gases are converted back to water, 
Water must be considered the byproduct of this reaction.
Stanley Meyer improved this inefficient burning process.
A one percent gain in efficiency of this combustion process.
Will far exceed the energy consumed to create Hydrogen and Oxygen from Water.       
R.I.P. Stanley Meyer..

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
I remember Medi with electroboom  doing a short clip on a roughly 3 meter loop with a white light emittting diode (LED) connecting the ends and completing the loop. And the loop laying on the floor.  Then using just one lead from a signal generator, he injected a 5mhz signal on either side of the LED, and cause it to light reguardless of which side the LED touched with the lead. 

I pointed out, using some math and  speed of light through cable, the cable length and given at 5 mhz with certain wavelength in a cable, that he was getting a voltage phase difference at the ends of the two leads by about (X) phase angle. And given that it takes about 3.4v to light a white LED, means his signal voltage was probably about (Y).

author: @garyhardman8369
text:
Many years ago, I demonstrated a rather extreme example of this phenomena to a colleague, whilst we were working in a high voltage substation.
Whilst unloading some test equipment from the van, my colleague reported feeling a 'buzzing sensation' when touching the van. I noticed that we were parked about 30 feet below the 400kV busbars...
I got my Fluke meter and measured nearly 1000 volts between a rusty spot on the van body and earth!

replies.author: @MrHistorian123
replies.text: Perfectly logical. The 400kV transmission line produces an electrical field which decreases the further you are away from it, so tapping between a point close to the cables and one further away will generate a large EMF between them.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text: As an aside, this makes me wonder if you could get useful energy from the electromagnetic radiation put out by the sun-  not with a photovoltaic, but with an antenna?   I'm sure someone's explored this, and solar cells are more efficient, but as it emits all sorts of frequencies , surely you could get something out of it?

replies.author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
replies.text: Why? The best way to capture useful energy from the electromagnetic radiation put out by the sun is photovoltaic. The majority of electromagnetic radiation put out by the sun is in the frequency spectrum we call light.

replies.author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
replies.text: @kensmith5694 it might but antennas are not very good at picking up power.  Just look how close your wireless phone charger has to be to work.

replies.author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
replies.text:
@kensmith5694 There’s a lot of thinking and not a lot of doing it’s not like radio is a new thing.  

Step back and have a think, the long wave band at say Radio 4 198Khz the transmitter at Droitwich output power is 500KW’s, at fifty miles away would you be able to light a 10W lamp from a tuned antenna? Would be nice to try or calculate the V/m at that distance. Also antennas have an impedance which you will need to match and manage to get all that power through.

Also the problem with radio waves is the atmosphere, it tends to reflect and/or attenuate RF. 

I hope I’m wrong, but it may take a change in physics to do it.

author: @mooneym.3642
text: I worked on harvesting radio for a while, fabricated the tiny circuit with diodes and caps attached to an antenna, even improved it a little by adding a grounded transformer which I think does the same thing you demonstrated by adding your body resistance. The microamps are good for powering a wall clock or trickle charging small batteries maybe if you build a bunch of them. Some sources said to use very large sheets of metal high above the ground but never tried those. Personally the only machine I ever fabricated that showed COP > 1 was a Bedini SSG monopole motor which I augmented with another idea floating out there in which you use a permanent magnet or two in the correct polarity at one or two ends of a cylindrical soft iron core transformer taking the unidirectional DC pulses from the Bedini ssg machine, the magnet (I used just one) did increase the output by a 100mA and since the waveform is all spikes reaching 90v then 0v its hard to find out the power. BUT it gave me an idea. What if we pulse unidirectional HV pulses through a transformer meant for HV (50k at least?) and then if the amps increased by a 100mA, that would give a workable amount of watts. Difficult to explain the magnet's augmentation thing since i forgot its name but what it does is it augments the strength of the magnetization of the transformer's core and since the north and south sides of the core will always remain north and south due to it being DC pulses, you just attach it to those ends in the correct polarity (south of a magnet to the north of the transformer's core and so on). Edit: Btw I never actually tried making an HV version because I have received electrical shocks so much and HV in particular scares me enough to not try it even in tiny "safe" amps like what is used for spark ignition in a plug for an automobile. Its quite shocking even if not painful and if the amps somehow hit the mark during experimentation...well.)

author: @newmonengineering
text: Also, it is fairly easy with things like a tesla coil or an rf transmitter to make a few watts and even power something with a little bit of knowledge. Transmitting a few watts of power wirelessly is not as hard as you think. Can easily transmit power a foot or so, and depending on the angle of the camera it's easy to hide the transmitter. I have seen so many videos like that, where you just know there is an external power source feeding thr system. Especially if the device is silly or so simple you just know it's not doing anything. Also mixing metals is how batteries work and it's easy to get a simple battery from some odds and ends. Don't fall for it. Just know if they have something, and I hope someone does one day, they should be open to testing and verifying the system.

author: @JoesWebPresence
text: Twenty years ago, I got very excited about powering an e-bike using the EM field, and had calculated that I'd need 72 small antennas to get the voltage I needed. A more electronically minded friend asked me what the ampage was, and I stared at him blankly. He switched my volt meter to a setting I was unfamiliar with, and after a small calculation announced that I'd need more than 5,000 of these antennas for every Amp required, putting an end to that dream.

replies.author: @kevinleebailey
replies.text: If you could get the antenni high enough it would have worked especially if it had acid etched points on it and some amalgam. You would have to step down the voltage via a spark gap and a coil transformer though.

replies.text: @kevinleebailey hundreds of feet high enough? I was working on a 15cm whip antenna and a false assumption. Imagine the size of bike I'd need to carry it all!

author: @grendel1960a
text: considering that earth potential on what is termed a hot site can be into several hundred volts (the reason its hot) special precautions are necessary for earthing within a radius of that site. (its all down to what is called step potential, ie the voltage difference between your two feet if you take a step. if you performed the same experiment as you did in the vicinity of such a site, the potential difference (voltage) could seem quite impressive. (note here step potential is an electrical infrastructure safety term, not a scientific term in this case.)
